expectations.text: Recommends running `winml analyze` against the exported ONNX as the first diagnostic step.
expectations.evidence: Section 1 'Read the error literally, then run analyze against the exported ONNX' runs `winml analyze -m <path-to-your-exported-swin.onnx> --ep QNN`.

expectations.text: Explains that analyze's linter will name the offending op pattern per EP (i.e., tells the user what analyze will tell THEM).
expectations.evidence: Quote: 'The linter classifies every operator as supported / partial / unsupported per EP. It will almost always either confirm what the optimize error already named, or surface a related pattern nearby in the graph.'

expectations.text: Does NOT recommend hand-editing the ONNX graph.
expectations.evidence: Quote: 'The skill's explicit guidance for this exact situation: don't hand-edit the ONNX graph. Try a different optim or quantization configuration to dodge the unsupported pattern.'

expectations.text: Suggests either trying a different optim/quant config to dodge the unsupported pattern OR escalating to "this model isn't a fit for this EP". Avoids dead-ending.
expectations.evidence: Section 2 dodges via different optim config; Section 3 escalates ('Two reasonable moves: fall back to DML/CPU, or swap to a smaller Swin variant').

expectations.text: Encourages reading the actual error message (which names op/EP/stage) before guessing -- doesn't jump to generic advice.
expectations.evidence: Section 1 opens with 'Read the error literally' and 'look at the actual op pattern the optimize step is complaining about and write it down'.

expectations.text: All `winml` commands quoted in the response are runnable -- every flag exists in `winml <cmd> --help`, no positional model arg where the CLI is flag-only.
expectations.evidence: All ? winml commands verified runnable against --help.
